planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist soil after night temperatures are reliably above 55°F; plants establish quickly in warm, humid conditions.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; in hot summer climates afternoon shade prevents leaf scorch and keeps the plant producing tender growth longer. Harvest young shoot tips and tender leaves regularly, which encourages bushy, compact regrowth; avoid consuming large quantities of raw katuk as it has been associated with lung issues when eaten raw in very high amounts — light cooking eliminates this concern. The protein-rich leaves are widely used in Southeast Asian cooking, where they have a pleasant, slightly sweet pea-like flavor. In zone 9, mulch heavily for winter protection; plants may die back but regrow from roots when warm weather returns.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Plant rhizome clumps in early spring; in zone 7 provide heavy mulch for winter protection as plants can be damaged below 20°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; this South African native is extremely drought tolerant once established and performs excellently in zone 9–10 gardens. The strap-like leaves and lavender flower clusters bloom nearly year-round in warm climates, making it one of the most reliable flowering perennials for the Gulf Coast. Leaves, flowers, and bulbs all have a mild garlic flavor without the lingering breath effect — giving rise to the common name — and are excellent as garlic substitutes in salads, stir-fries, and pestos. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; the plant spreads slowly by rhizome to form tidy clumps.

planting.note: Root 4–6 inch stem cuttings in water or moist mix after last frost; they root in 1–2 weeks and are ready to transplant when roots reach 1 inch.

directions: Root cuttings easily in water and transplant to fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade; the plant sprawls attractively as a groundcover or can be trained onto a low trellis. Harvest leaves and shoot tips regularly; the raw leaves have a crunchy, mild, slightly succulent texture and are popular in Southeast Asian salads and as an anti-diabetic traditional food. Unlike the closely related Okinawa spinach, this species has smooth (not wavy-edged) leaves without the purple underside. Bring cuttings indoors before first frost for overwintering; in frost-free areas it grows year-round and can be lightly sheared to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix after nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F; plants establish rapidly in warm weather.

directions: Plant cuttings in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in partial shade; this low-growing groundcover from Brazil spreads to fill in bare areas beneath shrubs and fruit trees, providing a living mulch while yielding edible leaves. The slightly succulent, wavy-edged leaves are harvested young and used raw in salads or briefly cooked like spinach, with a mild, pleasant flavor free of bitterness. It is extremely productive in the subtropics, rooting wherever stems contact moist soil. Take cuttings in early fall to overwinter indoors in a sunny window, or treat as an annual in zone 8 with fresh transplants each spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions 2–4 inches apart in spring or fall; in warm climates fall planting takes advantage of cooler temperatures for root establishment before summer heat.

directions: Plant divisions in virtually any soil type — from dry sandy to moderately moist — in full shade to partial sun; this tough Asian native is one of the most versatile shade groundcovers for southern gardens. Once established it smothers weeds, requires no mowing, and remains attractive year-round with its dark, grass-like evergreen foliage and small blue-black berries in fall. Mow or shear the entire planting to 2–3 inches in late winter every few years to remove older, ratty foliage and encourage fresh, uniform growth. Divide overgrown clumps every 4–5 years, replanting at the original spacing to maintain density.
